originContent: Spectator is a BEAM observer tool written in Gleam, that plays well with gleam_otp processes.
translatedContent: Spectator is a BEAM observer tool written in Gleam, that plays well with gleam_otp processes.

originContent: So far, spectator has the following features:
translatedContent: So far, spectator has the following features:

originContent: - Show processes in a sortable table
translatedContent: - Show processes in a sortable table

originContent: - Tag individual processes for easy identification
translatedContent: - Tag individual processes for easy identification

originContent: You can install spectator as a dependency in your project, and run it as part of your application to inspect your app in development.
translatedContent: You can install spectator as a dependency in your project, and run it as part of your application to inspect your app in development.
For details on how to do this, see the [package README](https://hexdocs.pm/spectator/)

originContent: For details on how to do this, see the [package README](https://hexdocs.pm/spectator/)

translatedContent: You can use spectator to inspect the BEAM node running your deployed application by running spectator as a standalone app, and connecting it to the erlang node your application is running on via distribution.

originContent: You can use spectator to inspect the BEAM node running your deployed application by running spectator as a standalone app, and connecting it to the erlang node your application is running on via distribution.

translatedContent: The standalone spectator application is available as a docker image via `ghcr.io/jonasgruenwald/spectator`, and as an escript via the [releases page](https://github.com/JonasGruenwald/spectator/releases).

originContent: The standalone spectator application is available as a docker image via `ghcr.io/jonasgruenwald/spectator`, and as an escript via the [releases page](https://github.com/JonasGruenwald/spectator/releases).

translatedContent: The repo contains [a full example of how to use spectator to inspect an app deployed via docker](https://github.com/JonasGruenwald/spectator/tree/main/example#readme).

originContent: The repo contains [a full example of how to use spectator to inspect an app deployed via docker](https://github.com/JonasGruenwald/spectator/tree/main/example#readme).

translatedContent: First, ensure your application has an Erlang name and cookie set, for example by setting the `ERL_FLAGS` environment variable before your application is started:

originContent: First, ensure your application has an Erlang name and cookie set, for example by setting the `ERL_FLAGS` environment variable before your application is started:

translatedContent: If your application is running in a docker container, put both your applications container, and the container running spectator on the same docker network, and ensure that your applications container has its hostname set to a value you know.

originContent: If your application is running in a docker container, put both your applications container, and the container running spectator on the same docker network, and ensure that your applications container has its hostname set to a value you know.

translatedContent: Start spectator, and click 'Change Target' in the top right corner of the application.

originContent: Start spectator, and click 'Change Target' in the top right corner of the application.

translatedContent: Enter the details of the node you wish to inspect, the name should be the name you set via `-sname`, followed by `@`, and then the hostname.

originContent: Enter the details of the node you wish to inspect, the name should be the name you set via `-sname`, followed by `@`, and then the hostname.

translatedContent: Finally, click 'Connect', to inspect the your application node.

originContent: Finally, click 'Connect', to inspect the your application node.

translatedContent: Note that the inspection target must have the same Erlang cookie set as spectator. Just like with your application, you can configure spectator's cookie via the `ERL_FLAGS` environment variable, e.g. `ERL_FLAGS="-name spectator@127.0.0.1 -setcookie mycookie"`.

originContent: Note that the inspection target must have the same Erlang cookie set as spectator. Just like with your application, you can configure spectator's cookie via the `ERL_FLAGS` environment variable, e.g. `ERL_FLAGS="-name spectator@127.0.0.1 -setcookie mycookie"`.

translatedContent: Please be aware of the following implications of running spectator:

originContent: Please be aware of the following implications of running spectator:

translatedContent: All displayed processes are probed in the configured interval using Erlang's `process_info/2` function which puts a temporary lock on the process being infoed. If the process is handling a lot of messages, this may have performance implications for the system

originContent: All displayed processes are probed in the configured interval using Erlang's `process_info/2` function which puts a temporary lock on the process being infoed. If the process is handling a lot of messages, this may have performance implications for the system

translatedContent: When you choose to connect to other Erlang nodes, spectator needs to convert the node name you provide into an atom. Therefore it is possible to exhaust the memory of the BEAM instance running spectator using its user interface, as atoms are never garbage collected.

originContent: When you choose to connect to other Erlang nodes, spectator needs to convert the node name you provide into an atom. Therefore it is possible to exhaust the memory of the BEAM instance running spectator using its user interface, as atoms are never garbage collected.

translatedContent: Spectator exposes sensitive details about the runtime it is inspecting via its web interface and does not currently gate access to it.

originContent: Spectator exposes sensitive details about the runtime it is inspecting via its web interface and does not currently gate access to it.
translatedContent: If you use spectator in a production environment, you are responsible for securing it – make sure to never expose spectator to the public internet.

originContent: If you use spectator in a production environment, you are responsible for securing it – make sure to never expose spectator to the public internet.
